title: Autumn: cooling the built-up pitta
excerpt: Every autumn, many people notice unexplained skin flare-ups, sharp hunger, night sweats, or a short fuse. Ayurveda has a precise name for what is happening, and a gentle seasonal plan to steady it.
body_html:
what happens to the body in autumn

Think about how the monsoon ends and the skies suddenly clear. The sun returns with a sharper bite, afternoons feel warmer than you expect for October, and your body has not quite made the adjustment. This is the season Ayurveda calls Sharad Ritu, roughly spanning September to November in the Indian subcontract. The classical texts mark it as the most important season for one particular problem: excess pitta.

Pitta is the dosha that governs heat, digestion, and transformation. During the monsoon your body has been working hard, and pitta accumulates quietly in the tissues. When the rains stop and the sun strengthens, that stored heat is released all at once. Caraka describes this process plainly: the body that has been cooled and dampened by Varsha Ritu is suddenly exposed to sharp solar radiation (atapa) in Sharad, provoking the pitta that was held in check (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Ashtanga Hridaya devotes a full chapter to Sharad ritucharya, listing the precise foods, timings, and behaviours that protect you during this surge (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3).

how pitta excess shows up day to day

You do not need to know the Sanskrit to recognise the signs. Skin that breaks out or turns red in autumn, a sharper-than-usual appetite that flips into acid reflux if you miss a meal, loose stools, waking between midnight and two in the morning, irritability that surprises even you: these are the classic presentations of accumulated pitta surfacing in Sharad. Sushruta Samhita notes that autumn is the season most associated with pittaja disorders, and that neglecting seasonal care at this time leaves a person vulnerable to fevers, bleeding conditions, and inflammatory skin complaints throughout the year (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana).

The good news is that pitta responds quickly to the right seasonal inputs. The changes needed are not dramatic: cooling foods, moderate quantities, earlier nights, and a few specific herbs. Within two to three weeks most people feel noticeably steadier.

the sharad ritucharya: simple seasonal habits

The cornerstone of autumn Ayurvedic care is what the texts call Sharad ritucharya, a set of seasonal observances. The word ritucharya simply means the conduct (charya) appropriate to the season (ritu). Ashtanga Hridaya recommends prioritising bitter, sweet, and astringent tastes in this season, all of which cool and balance pitta, while reducing salty, sour, and pungent foods that fan the fire (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 3). In practice this means favouring rice, moong dal, seasonal gourds, ripe sweet fruits like pomegranate and pear, and coriander-spiced preparations over tamarind-heavy curries, deep frying, and fermented pickles.

Coriander deserves a mention here: its seeds and fresh leaves are both recognised as pittashamaka (pitta-pacifying) in Bhavaprakasha Nighantu, and adding them liberally to food in autumn is one of the simplest, most evidence-aligned moves you can make. Fennel used in small quantities has a similar cooling, digestive quality.

Timings matter too. The classical advice is to eat the main meal at midday when digestive fire (agni) is highest, keep dinner light and early, and avoid eating after sunset, when pitta imbalance is most likely to manifest as reflux or disturbed sleep. Moonlight walks in the cool of the evening, something Caraka specifically mentions for Sharad (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana), are a gentle way to draw out the heat accumulated during the day. Avoid prolonged exposure to the midday sun, vigorous exercise during peak heat hours, and alcohol, all of which the texts identify as particularly aggravating in this season.

Virechana, the classical purification therapy using mild purgatives such as castor oil or triphala, is the seasonal panchakarma the texts prescribe for Sharad. This is mentioned in both Caraka Samhita (Kalpasthana) and Sushruta Samhita as the ideal Sharad intervention because it removes accumulated pitta through the gut, the path of least resistance. If you are interested in this, it should be done only under the supervision of a trained vaidya: self-administered strong purgation is not safe.

when to see a doctor

Ayurvedic seasonal care is preventive and supportive, not a treatment for active disease. If you have persistent high fever, significant rectal bleeding, severe acid reflux with chest pain, jaundice, or unexplained weight loss in autumn, please see a qualified doctor promptly. Inflammatory skin conditions covering a large area, deep infections, or sudden sharp vision changes also need medical evaluation, not dietary adjustment alone. A qualified vaidya can help you personalise a ritucharya plan, but this article is general information only and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for professional medical care.

Try This Today

  • Coriander water in the morning: Soak one teaspoon of coriander seeds in a glass of water overnight. Strain and drink it first thing on an empty stomach. It is a gentle, classical pitta-cooling ritual that takes ten seconds to prepare.
  • Switch to moong dal for dinner: Replace heavier lentils (urad, rajma, chana) with yellow moong dal at the evening meal through October and November. Moong is specifically listed as the ideal grain-pulse for Sharad in Ashtanga Hridaya.
  • Eat your largest meal at noon: Shift the heaviest eating to midday, when solar energy mirrors your digestive fire at its peak. Keep dinner small and eat it before 7 p.m. to avoid overnight pitta-driven reflux and broken sleep.
  • An evening walk under open sky: A 20-minute stroll after sunset, when the air is cooler, is specifically mentioned in classical texts as a Sharad practice. It helps the body release heat absorbed during the day without stimulating further pitta.
  • Cut the souring agents: Reduce tamarind, excessive lemon, vinegar-based pickles, and fermented preparations through the season. These are rasas (tastes) that aggravate pitta and are flagged in both Caraka and Ashtanga Hridaya as particularly problematic in Sharad.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Coriander (धनिया)

Pittashamaka (pitta-cooling): both seeds and fresh leaves are used in autumn food preparation to pacify accumulated heat.

Fennel (सौंफ)

Mildly sweet and cooling; supports digestion without heating, making it ideal for the Sharad season when pitta is high.

metafields.dek: Every autumn, many people notice unexplained skin flare-ups, sharp hunger, night sweats, or a short fuse. Ayurveda has a precise name for what is happening, and a gentle seasonal plan to steady it.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Sharad Ritu (autumn) is the peak season for pitta accumulation; the sun returning after monsoon releases stored heat into the body.
Bitter, sweet, and astringent tastes, cooler mealtimes, and lighter dinners are the classical first-line response.
Virechana (mild purgation) is the season's prescribed cleanse, but should only be done with professional guidance.

glossary_terms.definition: The autumn season in the Ayurvedic calendar, roughly September to November, considered the peak pitta season.
The dosha associated with heat, digestion, and transformation; it governs metabolism, skin, and sharp mental focus.
Seasonal conduct: the diet, lifestyle, and therapeutic routines prescribed for each of the six Ayurvedic seasons.
Pitta-pacifying; used to describe foods and herbs that cool and balance excess pitta.
Classical purification therapy using mild purgation to remove accumulated pitta via the digestive tract; the seasonal cleanse for Sharad.
Digestive fire; the principle governing how food is transformed into nourishment. Balanced agni is central to health in every season.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ritucharya adhyaya (seasonal regimen and pitta accumulation in Sharad)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ritucharya adhyaya (Sharad seasonal conduct: tastes, foods, and behaviours for autumn)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (pittaja disorders and seasonal vulnerability in Sharad)
Bhavaprakasha, Bhavaprakasha Nighantu (pittashamaka properties of coriander and fennel)
Caraka Samhita, Siddhisthana (virechana as the seasonal purification therapy for Sharad)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Sharad Ritu" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Sharad Ritu is defined as: The autumn season in the Ayurvedic calendar, roughly September to November, considered the peak pitta season.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to coriander water in the morning. Soak one teaspoon of coriander seeds in a glass of water overnight. Strain and drink it first thing on an empty stomach. It is a gentle, classical pitta-cooling ritual that takes ten seconds to prepare.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Coriander for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Coriander is highly regarded. Pittashamaka (pitta-cooling): both seeds and fresh leaves are used in autumn food preparation to pacify accumulated heat.
